self_report.reflection: Substituting t=u^2 yields 2∫₀^∞ e^{-a(u^2+u^{-2})}du = 2e^{-2a}∫₀^∞ e^{-a(u-u^{-1})^2}du. The Cauchy–Schlömilch involution u↦1/u combined with v = u - 1/u (so dv = (1+u^{-2})du, v ranges over ℝ) gives ∫₀^∞ e^{-a(u-u^{-1})^2}du = (1/2)√(π/a). Therefore the integral equals √(π/a)·e^{-2a}, matching the claim exactly.
self_report.notes: Standard Putnam 1985 B-5 with the Glasser/Cauchy–Schlömilch trick; the closed form √(π/a)e^{-2a} is the textbook answer.
